rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient is systemically well (afebrile, no vomiting, no Kussmaul breathing, alert, tolerating fluids) with a Risk-Level-Low presentation: symptomatic hyperglycaemia in the pre-diabetic range plus a localised candidal intertrigo. This needs prompt attention — fasting venous bloods arranged for the next morning, a topical antifungal, and a GP review within 24–48 hours to assess the rash and results — but not urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3), an ED visit (T4) or a 000 ambulance (T5). Per the T4/T5 ceiling discipline, there is no patient-reportable time-critical emergency feature, so T2 (prompt GP) is the conservative-but-appropriate tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The groin redness spreads rapidly down the leg or beyond the fold, the area becomes hot, hard and swollen, or a fever above 38 C develops
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately, at any point — do not wait for the GP appointment
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate a severe secondary bacterial infection / spreading cellulitis with sepsis risk, which is an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading quickly down your leg, the area turns hot, hard and swollen, or you spike a fever over 38 degrees, go to the emergency department or call 000 straight away — that means the infection has turned serious.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting, deep or rapid breathing, drowsiness or confusion, severe abdominal pain, fruity-smelling breath, or being unable to keep fluids down
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of a hyperglycaemic emergency (DKA or hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state), which is life-threatening and needs an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start vomiting, breathing fast and deep, feel drowsy or confused, get bad tummy pain, or can't keep fluids down, call 000 immediately — that can mean your blood sugar has become dangerously high.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rash is no better or worse after 48 hours of antifungal treatment, or the fatigue and urinary symptoms worsen despite the plan
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within 24 hours of noticing no improvement
escalation_edges.rationale: Treatment failure or symptomatic worsening warrants an urgent same-day in-person review to reassess the rash (possible bacterial superinfection) and glycaemic status.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rash hasn't started improving in a couple of days on the cream, or you feel worse, book an urgent same-day appointment to be seen in person.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No emergency or treatment-failure features — routine progression of the plan
escalation_edges.rationale: Baseline pathway: fasting bloods tomorrow morning and GP review within 24–48 hours to check the rash and results.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Otherwise, get your fasting bloods done first thing tomorrow and see your GP within the next day or two to check the rash and go over the results.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and called out of hours; no one else present to monitor deterioration overnight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Make the emergency triggers concrete and easy to self-check, and confirm the patient can call 000 and reach pathology/the GP independently the next day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Undiagnosed impaired glucose regulation with glycosuria predisposing to infection
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for re-contact if the skin infection changes character, given impaired glucose control slows healing and raises bacterial-superinfection risk.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Get a fasting blood test (fasting glucose, HbA1c and lipids) first thing tomorrow morning — nothing but water from midnight — and see your GP within the next day or two to review the rash and the results. Use the antifungal cream twice a day and keep the area clean and dry. Stop sugary drinks now and try a short walk after your biggest meal.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a GP appointment within 24–48 hours; attend the pathology collection centre tomorrow morning.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If any emergency signs appear overnight — spreading hot swollen redness or fever, or vomiting/deep breathing/drowsiness — call 000; otherwise the fasting bloods and GP review are next-day, not overnight, tasks.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth assessment of symptomatic hyperglycaemia (impaired-glucose-regulation pattern with insulin-resistance stigmata) and candidal intertrigo. Fasting glucose/HbA1c/lipids requested; topical antifungal advised; lifestyle counselling given; GP to confirm glycaemic category, treat/monitor the rash, review blood pressure and consider a GPMP.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as pure self-care with no arranged testing and no safety-netting, dismissing the symptomatic hyperglycaemia and the skin infection.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Missed progression to frank diabetes or an unrecognised deterioration of the skin infection into cellulitis/sepsis, or an undetected hyperglycaemic emergency.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Failing to arrange any glycaemic testing or skin treatment and giving no follow-up plan.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a stable, systemically-well pre-diabetes presentation to the emergency department or calling a 000 ambulance — unnecessary emergency resource use for a Risk-Level-Low metabolic screening presentation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the patient has pre-diabetes or has already crossed into frank type 2 diabetes cannot be resolved via telehealth — it needs a fasting venous glucose and HbA1c.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the metabolic pattern as suspected (not confirmed), arrange the fasting labs, treat the skin infection, give lifestyle advice and safety-net — deferring the definitive glycaemic label to the laboratory result.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either declaring frank diabetes (or starting a hypoglycaemic drug) on a single random reading, or falsely reassuring the patient that no testing is needed.
